content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Manic Pixie Dream Girl movies that do the trope right

**Score:** 77
**Body:** I just finised Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind (2004) again and loved it as usual. It got me thinking of the Manic Pixie Dream Girl trope and of doing a binge of some of the best.

I'm thinking Garden State (2004), followed by 500 Days of Summer (2009), and then Scott Pilgrim Vs the World (2010). I am a big fan of all these movies, but would like to find a couple more than I haven't seen. I would prefer choices that are genuinely good. I know the trope gets a bad rap so I would like to avoid the sucky ones that people hate

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Almost Famous (2000)
2. Breakfast at Tiffany's, What's Up Doc? - start with the classics. 
  - And Bringing Up Baby
3. Harold and Maude
  - This is strangely the best answer 
4. The Fifth Element. Leeloo is a SUPERPOWERED MPDG.
  - Also exemplifies the “born sexy yesterday” trope. Love that movie tho
5. Ruby Sparks (2012)
6. Amálie, even though she's the main character. 


The Perks of Being a Wallflower


(In book form, Clarisse in Fahrenheit 451, but not so much in either film)
7. Annie Hall

## 2. The Last Stop in Yuma County

**Score:** 25
**Body:** Just watched The Last Stop in Yuma County and wow, what a tense, gripping ride! I haven’t seen many people recommending this one—I kinda stumbled upon it myself. If you’re into tight, tense, low-budget crime thrillers this is a real hidden gem from 2023

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I liked it a lot.  I'm a sucker for "people trapped in one location in a stressful situation" stories.

If you liked it, check out Bad Times at the El Royale, which is another neo-noir period piece with a slightly larger physical radius, but not by much.  
  - Thanks for the suggestion! I guess I’ve got something to watch on Sunday now. 😄
    - Identity, The Hateful Eight, and The Mist also fit into this category.

Snake Eyes (the Nicolas Cage movie, not the G.I. Joe movie) does too, just more people in a larger place.
      - Does this genre have a name? I love these type of movies too, do you have any more recommendations, as I've not seen a couple of yours, so will have something to watch! :)
2. Same here. I stumbled onto it. Really liked this one. It reminded me of the tic tight well written black and white noirs of the 1950s. Felt like I was waiting for Lee Marvin, Rod Steiger or Sterling Hayden to show up. lol. If these independent noir crime shows are up your alley I would suggest How To Blow Up A Pipeline and catch The Fair One. 
3. All of Jim Cummings (the good one) movies are great.

Beta Test and Thunder Road he is incredible in. Wolf of Snow Hollow is fun ride. And he has a cameo in Greener Grass, which is one of the most out-there comedies you’ll ever see.
4. Thought it was good but idk wanted more from it too

Guess the budget was plenty limited 
  - this is how i felt about Strange Darling, mostly b/c i clocked the gimmick almost straightaway and hoped there was more to it.

Yuma is perfect, IMO.  perfect setup & execution, and that final act i didn't see coming at all - but it fits perfectly and it's not cheap at all
5. This is a very good and well praised movie.  Got the director an Evil Dead movie which he is currently filming.  Evil Dead Wrath
6. Saw it based off Jim Cummings’ works (Thunder Road, The Wolf of Snow Hollow, The Beta Test.)

I see he starred in something called Redux Redux last year that’s gotten intriguing reviews. 

## 3. Bittersweet coming of age movies

**Score:** 19
**Body:** 
My favourite movie is definitely The Perks Of Being A Wallflower. I also really loved The Fallout (not to be confused with the show Fallout), and Matilda, even tho it's a kids movie.

I love teenage/coming of age movies a lot, especially with a very strong focus on friendship or found family.
In all honesty most of my favourite movies are focused on teenagers learning to live again after trauma, whether it was recent trauma or from long ago, so I think I'd probably enjoy more movies like that.

LGBTQ+ movies would also be really good, especially ones with trans main characters. I also really enjoy a modern manic pixie dream girl trope given that it's done well, but that's obviously not a requirement.




### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Way Way Back is one of my all time favorite movies

St Vincent might fit this too 
2. Stand By Me
3. i wish you all the best 

you can live forever

the edge of seventeen

lady bird 

lost & delirious

short term 12 
4. - Welcome to the Dollhouse (1995) from Todd Solondz.
- My Life as a Dog (1985) from Lasse Hallström.
5. Now and Then 
6. Eighth Grade is my favorite. Lady Bird and Booksmart are also great. And for 20s-30s coming of age, I love Worst Person in the World and Frances Ha. 
7. Kiki's Delivery Service

Muriel's Wedding

The Miseducation of Cameron Post

Water Lilies

Girl Interrupted
  - Came here to say Miseducation of Cameron Post.
8. My girl
9. The Outsiders (1983)

What's Eating Gilbert Grape (1993)

Secondhand Lions (2003)

Pride (2014) - Not really a coming of age movie in the traditional sense, but it does share some similar themes. It's a really good LGBTQ film that's based on a true story, and definitely worth watching.

## 4. Movies that stuck with you / What should I watch next?

**Score:** 16
**Body:** Hi r/movies,

I’m looking for movie recommendations and thought this might be the best way to find some good ones.

I’ll watch pretty much anything any genre, any decade, popular or obscure. I’m especially interested in movies that really stuck with you after watching.

Basically trying to escape the endless scrolling phase where I open a streaming service, stare at it for 20 minutes, and then give up.

Hit me with your favorites!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Basketball Diaries - My all time favorite Leo Dicaprio movie. It's the acting of Leo that made me stuck with it. Now, everytime I watch a movie I use him as my benchmark to gauge the acting skills of actors.

Bogonia - Don't really want to spoil it but I guarantee you'll watch it more than once

Her - I find this the most impactful especially with the emergence of AI and how vulnerable people can be attached to someone or something that shows them affection.

2. City of God (2002)

It's a movie everyone should watch, at least once.
3. In the Mood for Love

4. I'd suggest Amélie (2001)... it's classified as a romance/comedy but is really (also) a remarkably quirky art film with unexpected twists and engaging wry wit. It's a French film but the subtitles are excellent. Even more enjoyable if you've got some familiarity with French. The lead's character and perspective on life will stick with you.
5. 1.American History X
2.Fight Club
3.Shawshank Redemtion
4.The dark Knight
5.Goodfellas
6.Saving Privare Rayan
7.Gladiator
8.Leon
9.Dune 2
6. Coherence  
12 Monkeys  
The Man from Earth  
Arrival  
Interstellar  
Caddo Lake
  - Bro these are goated brooo


  - Specially coherence , it is so underrated and its concept has way too much potential and can get even more


7. - The Broken Circle Breakdown (2012)
- The Great Beauty (2013)
- Incendies (2010)
- Dancer in the Dark (2010)
8. There are so many! Maybe start with the classics/greats. Or film directors you like. Or check out what films won at film festivals (Cannes, Toronto, Sundance…).

I’ll give you one recommendation: Citizen Kane.

## 5. "Time travel" heartfelt stories recommendations?

**Score:** 18
**Body:** Looking for stories that talk about the concept of time and its relation to humanity or very personal human experiences, bonus points if it is heartfelt in a way. Some examples I've been thinking of are When Marnie Was There (2015), Caddo Lake (2024), Lake Mungo (2008), Arrival (2016). 

Any genre. Shows could work too. Doesn't have to be "time travel" to a T, I just don't know how else to define this type of story. Sorry if it's vague, but if any movie remotely reminds you of this description let me know, I'd love to hear it

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. About Time (2013) touches on this theme and is heartfelt. 
2. You might like About Time. It starts like a quirky time travel movie then slowly turns into a really emotional story about family and appreciating normal moments. Sneaks up on you in the best way.
3. Safety not guaranteed with Aubrey plaza 
4. Somewhere in Time
5. Peggy Sue Got Married 
6. Time Travelers Wife with Eric Bana and Rachel McAdams.  The book is excellent. 
7. The Girl Who Leapt Through Time. Its an anime movie but since you mentioned Marnie, its definitely  worth checking out. Same studio(Studio Chizu) did Summer Wars, Bell, Wolf Children, Mirai, and The Boy & The Beast.
8. There is a movie from 1980 called Somewhere in Time. It not only includes time travel, it includes matters of the heart. It is with Christopher Reeves and Jane Seymour. Yes, it's an old one but still a good one. 
9. Frequency 
10. You’re going to LOVE about time. You need to go and watch it right now 

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Does any actor need special conditions to be able to shoot a scene?

**Score:** 2736
**Body:** In the movie Starship Troopers there was a nude scene in the shower. All the recruits, male and female, were completely naked in the shower. The atmosphere of the scene is one of camaraderie, not erotic. The scene was intended to symbolize equality among recruits and to represent a futuristic and open-minded society.

Therefore, the scene is quite relevant to the plot of the science fiction film. However, the actors demanded a special condition in exchange for filming it nude. They would only act in the scene if Paul Verhoeven, the director, was also naked in the process of shooting.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He did the same thing in Robocop. Police locker room was unisex.
2. "You sons of bitches, I'm in." - Paul Verhoeven
  - Jokes on you, I'm into that shit

![gif](giphy|NQkOFaFR5WMIzY9Zp7)
3. Dizzie😍
4. In the movie This Is Where I Leave You, Dax Shepard had a condition for his sex scene. They could show his butt, but not his butthole. In the scene the camera pans across the room, and a flower (if i remember correctly) on the table is at the perfect angle to cover his butthole. He said they had to film the scene a bunch of times because the angle wasnt right and you could see his butthole.
  - As result his butthole was the most discussed topic among the film crew and would have not even be noticed otherwise 
5. You have the story slightly mixed up.  The actors were hesitant to shoot the communal shower scene as written and Verhoeven didn't understand their hangups about nudity.  Dina Meyer sort of joked that it was easy for him to say, so he volunteered to direct the scene similarly undressed (he wasn't completely naked but rather just dropped his pants when goaded by Meyers).  It wasn't really a demand or condition made by the cast.

[The real story behind Starship Troopers director Paul Verhoeven removing his own pants when filming the memorable shower scene](https://www.thepopverse.com/movies-starship-troopers-paul-verhoeven-jost-vacano-naked-filming-shower-scene-dina-meyer-c2e2)
6. What's weird is they had not even made their demands and there he was buck naked as soon as he walked in.

/s
7. IIRC there was a photo shoot with Kiera Knightly where the photographer wanted her to be topless. She agreed, but only under the condition that her body was not altered in anyway in the finished project. (Airbrushed, photoshopped, etc.)
8. Tobias can never be nude. 

## 2. I have watched the movie Contact starring Jodie Foster many times. I really like it a lot. But when I recommended it to my friends, none of them liked it. Have you watched the movie Contact? How did you like it?

**Score:** 731

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Classic. Love it
  - Hated it as a kid who couldn’t wait to see what aliens look like

Loved it as an adult facing the ambiguity of life 
2. I liked the movie, but I didn't like how they leaned into "faith" when the author, Sagan, was a well known atheist and the book didn't go that direction at all.
  - I always believed Sagan wrote himself into Jodie Fosters character and put his own strain living as a ‘science guy’ in a ‘world of faith’ on the page. 
    - The book is Sagan’s take on if God was real, he would be indistinguishable from an alien.

Atheists tend to hate that Sagan was so charitable in the book, because the book embraces that premise fully.
  - If anything it was more science grounded in my opinion. Especially the end when the guy interrogating her that says that they can't accept her experience based on faith. Which was a throwback to when they chose drumlin based on his faith. It kind of shows that their faith is just based on what they want more than anything. Also how drumlin just told them what they wanted to hear and that he admitted to taking advantage of her.
    - Tom Skerrit was so great as a total prick in this. He really made you loathe Drumlin but not maybe outright hate him. And (spoiler) he dies bravely enough you kind of miss him after. Top-drawer performance from him in a film with a lot of great performances.
3. I watched it when it was first released and really enjoyed it. I haven’t seen it in a long time, and my views are more critical now, so I’m curious to see how it holds up. Thanks for the post I will re-watch it.
4. I like the movie to this day. It does make Sagan's story a bit more palpable to general audiences, but if you read the original novel, it has its share of story beats that do differ and go into more of the science fiction aspect.
5. I didn’t get it when I first watched it many years ago. Recently I rewatched it and got what it meant by her father’s show up. Now it’s one of my best alien movies 

## 3. Diedrich Bader is fucking hilarious.

**Score:** 672

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. If you didn't grow up watching THE DREW CAREY SHOW then you're honestly missing out. Up until the actress who played Kate left to do Scrubs, that show was hilarious.
  - That’s a cast that always felt like they loved each other 
2. ![gif](giphy|lOrD9MitzQK09dzoJV|downsized)

Bow to your sensei!
  - "Grab my arm... the other arm... *my* other arm."
3. ![gif](giphy|Wpz1Hl1BqlaMw)
4. “Don’t worry Peter I won’t tell anybody” 

“He’s cool” 
  - Who the fuck is that?!
5. He’s a great Jethro in The Beverly Hillbillies movie 
6. https://preview.redd.it/32ed7lhznqng1.jpeg?width=304&format=pjpg&auto=webp&s=2830da47e53324d5f43187f36bde9bcfe19b5400

Oh, what a lovely tea party
7. Hey Peter!


Watch out for your cornhole.

## 4. Favourite movie where, if you just left the main character alone, thousands of lives would be saved.

**Score:** 336

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. ![gif](giphy|utWeADSojWxO0)
  - "Yeah well he stole John Wick's car and killed his dog."

"OH. " yuuup. F.A.F.O
    - A fucking pencil...
2. https://i.redd.it/9jw73lbqwsng1.gif

John Matrix
3. It’s always funny how Rambo went from a symbol of civic rebellion against a brutal US government to a tool of US governtment propaganda.
  - And from a warning about the trauma of going to war turned into the hero of going back to war. 
4. No civilians died in Rambo First Blood.
5. Only the one cop who fell from the copter dies in FB. 


6. Star wars. If Owen and Beru weren't killed Luke probably would have stuck around and not blown up the death star. Tark8lin would have blown up Alderaan anyway.
7. John Wick

## 5. Paul Mescal, Jesse Plemons, and Dwayne Johnson all gave exceptional performances this year and none of them got nominated. Why do you think that is?

**Score:** 319

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I know Jesse Plemons is a great actor because I have no idea what his actual personality looks like 
  - He was great in Game Night
    - How can that be profitable for Frito-Lay?
  - He scared the shit out of me in Breaking Bad
2. Plemons was robbed
  - Thank you. Been saying this since I saw Bugonia. The two standout scenes >! with him at the hospital trying to save his mom, and the scene where he returns home and is confronted by Emma as he tries to process what just happened !< was incredible. The guy is so talented man. It’s ridiculous. 
    - When he’s just pedalling so hard in the blood-soaked outfit, it makes me fucking hoot in laughter. 
3. Joel Edgerton for *Train Dreams* was my own #1 "shoulda been nominated." But he's always underrated—one of those protean actors who truly vanish into the character. Almost ten years ago, the Academy nominated his co-star Ruth Negga for *Loving* and not him, and his was the great performance in that movie, IMO.  
  - Came here to mention Edgerton for Train Dreams.
4. Dwayne was nominated for a Golden Globe. Plemmons was nominated for SAG and a BAFTA. 

If you're asking why didn't Dwayne Johnson get nominated for an Oscar you're insane. Not every great performance can get nominated, especially in a year with as many great ones as last year. But the Rock wasn't very good. He was just good for The Rock.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Watching Good Time (2017) made me understand why I didn't like Marty Supreme (2025) all that much.

**Score:** 131
**Body:** One of the biggest problems I have with Marty Supreme was that it feels aimless. Multiple loose ended threads run throughout the film but instead of feeling like they aid the characters they feel unsatisfactory and empty. One of the main examples of this is the dog sequence which feels quite redundant, almost clownish to the point that it might be a parody of other Safdie films.

Good Time also has loose ends but ones that are able to service the character. Robert Pattinson's girlfriend in the movie has a very short screen time but the character doesn't feel wasted the way Gwenyth Paltrow's character does. Pattinson is supposed to be a selfish career criminal who wants to keep hustling and hustling manipulating everyone who he comes across especially marginalized people. Chalamet's character is also similar. Neither of them are sympathetic yet I am more immersed in Pattinson than I am in Chalamet. I don't care for Pattinson's character; I don't root for him, I don't care if he goes to jail but I am curious to see what he does next. This similar sense of urgency is not there for Marty, only an understanding that this guy is so driven by ambition that he will do anything (and anyone) for it. He doesn't make me curious. The end, however you interpret it, didn't leave me satisfied because I didn't buy Marty's character very well. 

In [this review](https://letterboxd.com/film/good-time/reviews/by/activity/) of Good Time, the author mentions the film editor Mark Peranson's love letter to Cannes and how he describes Good Time as "a kind of Dionysian New York Gesamtkunstwerk....immersion without identification." A quality I found lacking in Marty Supreme even though, in my opinion, it needed it.

I think it also helps that Good Time looks very good. Way better than Marty Supreme. I like the way the glitzy nighttime city is portrayed where the weak, marginalized and the wary are beaten down. The beautiful close up shots of the character's face that allows the actor to showcase his range, the subtle but energetic acting by Robert Pattinson and very importantly a short runtime all add a lot to the experience. If Marty Supreme was a good 30-40 minutes shorter I think I would have enjoyed the film more. 

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Important distinction is that although Pattinson's character is incredibly selfish, the entire emotional crux of the film is he is trying to save his disabled brother. That alone gives it infinitely more weight and stakes than Marty.

Also I actually felt like RP was playing a character in a story. Couldn't shake the vibe that MS was mostly watching 2.5 hrs of Chalemet more or less playing himself. Almost like there were no stakes even for the actor as there was no plumbing the depths to emerge with something new.

That's not to disparage his skill as I've thought he was great in most other roles. MS was merely very entertaining but left no impression on me
  - It also helps that emotional payoff comes from the brother’s revelation in the credits. We spend the entire film watching a sociopath drag everyone around him down, because he’s incapable of holding himself accountable. It finds a way to both condemn Connie for his actions and grant the audience some catharsis with a different character.

I feel like Marty’s entire final act has very little attachment to the rest of the film. To which I guess that’s mainly to OP’s point, the film feels quite scattered. 

Uncut Gems felt the most concise to me. Every action rippling down the stream, creating some larger consequence. I get why Josh stepped back for several years, it’s hard to top something like that.
    - Honestly _Uncut Gems_ is really the perfect realization of the Safdie vision. 

MS seemed like a bloated rehash, and adding that extra budget and scope actually hurts it rather than helps it. 

I also think that Bennie not being part of it hurt it - there’s no adult in the room saying “hey cut this, hey this is superfluous and self indulgent, hey this dog thread feels hackneyed and random at best” etc etc. 

There are great parts of MS. I think if you trimmed it down it would be better (basically toss the dog plot and the central
park scene, probably some others). But even then I just didn’t find the broader story, characters, or performances that compelling. Odessa A’zion was more impressive than anyone to me, and she seems like the least emphasized character, ironically. 

_Uncut Gems_ remains the Safdie GOAT and I’m not sure they’ll ever be able to recapture  that lightning in a bottle. I don’t really perceive them as a dynamic duo like the Coens with a very versatile style - I think anxiety pressure cooker city sleaze only has so many applications.
    - Uncut Gems made me so uncomfortable the first time I watched it, great movie
  - THANK YOU. The brother in Good Time is why it works. The Safdies saw the praise and used the same outline for the rest of their movies, and have not been able to make it work. 
2. I support you on this, but I think a better comparison is uncut gems. To me, everyone in MS is so unlikeable, the movie has zero heart and the only person I felt anything for was the baby at the end, for having such shitty parents. 
Yes sandler was just as narcissistic, but his family were likeable, even his mistress had his best intentions at heart. So I cared about him and what was going on, even though he was his own worst enemy. MS had none of that, and I disliked the film because of it.

  - The characters were compelling nonetheless. Interest > likability 
  - I did not find those characters in Uncut to be more likeable than the ones in MS, who also all arguably have good intentions. 

Sandler's wife is relentlessly cold, his father in law has sicked dangerous thugs on him, and his mistress (now his girlfriend, it appears), is secretly seeing another man.

Not to say Sandler's character deserves better than that, but compared to MS the characters are no more likeable imo. Maybe more interesting?

3. Pattinson is the better actor in a better film.

Its a better written film, carried out with better acting, and better directing,  its as simple as that.

Chalamet is a decent actor, but hes not on Pattinsons level yet.
4. I liked the movie but didnt love it.

MS is definitely following a Safdie formula: where Good Time was “lets put a ‘sleep is the cousin of death’ NewYawk hustlerbro archetype in the frying pan of what the matrix of poverty and the criminal justice system actually looks loke”, Marty Supreme is like “lets put a frustrated working-class Ayn Randian ‘superman’ into the frying pan of the American class system calcifying into a permanent caste system.”

Whether that was the intent, having an essentially rightwing New York American Jew in the 1950s coming from a milieu of holocaust survivors and trying to court titans of industry and proximity to power, the character Marty feels very much like a send up of the hyper-rational, world-molding, unequalled Hero archetype in Ayn Rand’s work. 

Whether i “picked up” on this early on or just head canon’d myself, I definitely felt like all of his decisions and the setbacks of the plot supported this framing. 

I think they did a poor job of teaching the audience what the Marty’s character is going to be like and so there was an expectation of character growth among a lot of viewers. And its like “no no this is a type of person who says to themselves ‘why should i change when its everyone else who sucks?’” I blame no one for not wanting to spend 150 minutes with the guy. 

I do get your issues with the pacing, and feel like it was intentional in that it mirrors a kind of post-war meandering realist novel someone in the NY intelligentsia might write. Vs. like a true-crime ny hiphop song.

Also theres a lot of “but its the 1950s, look its analog” in the film. I think they screwed up by keeping the soundtrack vaporwave adjacent instead of a more on-the-nose jazz/bop sountrack which might have cued the audience that it was an earlier era where processes might naturally take longer…but would still fall apart disastrously in a manner of seconds.


## 2. Films elevated by their final scene

**Score:** 92
**Body:** There's something about these movies that just turn it up at the ending. 

Example: I love Magnolia and I think the final scene and look into the camera moves it from a great movie to one of the best of all time. Throughout the movie everyone is having a shit time and there is not a lot telling us that things will improve. Then we get that final scene, with Aimee Mann's "Save Me" playing as John C Reilly tells Claudia that he's willing to take a bet on her. She smiles, and gives the camera a knowing look. There is a slimmer of hope there. It's absolutely gorgeous and moves the plot in a positive direction at the final point, while still feeling wholly deserved. 

In a similar (and recept) vein, Marty Supreme's final scene switches things up to let us hope that he might become a better man.

So I guess my thread here is partly to gush about Magnolia, but also to ask you guys: What are your films that go from good go great "thanks to" the final scene?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A recent one in memory is The Zone of Interest, elevated from a great movie to a masterpiece. Fitting that the particular scene also makes the point that the events of now are not too dissimilar from the events of the past. 

Sentimental Value was another one, but rather than reframing what preceded the final scene, it delivered on what was gradually built up as the film went along.
2. **Chinatown** feels like you’re watching the story of some detective getting to the bottom of a mystery to accomplish some semblance of justice. The ending reveals that this isn’t a story about seeking truth or justice, but showing how those in power don’t bend to truth and will never be held accountable for their injustice.

That end scene completely redefines the movie.

  - Forget it, Jake
3. *Beau Travail* is the first that comes to mind as a very good movie with a *perfect* ending. It's a brilliant moment of characterization, in that it raises to the surface everything that's been repressed in Galoup and recontextualizes all his prior behavior. But it's also overpowering purely as an audio-visual experience. The choice of song, the contrast between the smoky dance floor and the arid landscapes that dominate the rest of the film, even the timing of the cuts to the credits. It's one of the first scenes I think of as an example of what cinema can do that other mediums can't. 
4. The Lives of Others (2006). Really great German film about Stasi surveillance in East Berlin in the 80s. Wonderful movie but the final scene is just beautiful. I burst into tears.

Cinema Paradiso (1988). Another great film but the ending is truly beautiful. If you know, you know.
5. "The Florida Project".  I would say that every scene in the film is great, but the final scene — especially the final shot — was a capper that was both beautiful and horrifyingly real. The theme of children having to manage themselves and maintain hope in a paved and plastic emotional desert created by adults is pervasive, but that final moment is such a gut punch because it juxtaposed the hope and boundless energy of childhood with seduction into a trap engineered to convince people of all ages that lies are truth.
6. The ending of ‘Being There’ is the prime example in my opinion. It completely recontextualizes all that comes before.  


It elevates the satire into something spiritual. Without the ending, Being There is a brilliant, cynical comedy about media, politics, and elite self-delusion. The ending cracks that cynicism open.
7. Recent one for me is Hamnet, just watched it yesterday. I was enjoying the movie for most of the run time but it wasn't blowing me away or anything. Then the final scene happened and it all clicked for it. It was movie magic. I loved it.
8. The last 30 seconds of Enemy change the entire context of the movie up until that point. I've only seen it once but I'd never be able to watch it the same way again after the twist right at the end.
9. Melancholia's ending scene elevated the film for me; the scene also stayed with me. I even wrote an [Interpretation](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/comments/1pmgg1l/lvts_melancholia_ending_and_climax_my/) on the final scene.

## 3. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (March 08, 2026)

**Score:** 7
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **The Hitcher (1987)**

This was a delightfully tense cinema watch -- extraordinarily homoerotic 90% of the time, which I am always a fan of. Both of the mains were really good and the australian desert almost becomes a character in and off itself in this, which is pretty cool. 


**Marty Supreme (2025)**

This finally made it to cinemas here and I thought it was good. The only other Safdie film I've seen is Uncut Gems, which I thought suffered from similar things that this one does -- lots of ideas that just feel like they're thrown against the wall to see what sticks. It's past the second half where this one really starts shining, both the music and the action on screen finally converge properly and I'm finally starting to get hyped and then it's suddenly over. 

**The Curve (1998)**

This was very silly and very watchable as far as 90s off-brand "teen horror" goes. this doesn't take itself too seriously in the least and embraces its concept to the full (which I described as bisexual Heathers meets the SNL "Dear Sister" sketch on Letterboxd lol)

**Shaun of the Dead (2004)**

Another great watch in the cinema. I could have sworn I've seen it before, but halfway through the film I realized I probably didn't and was just conflating it with the rest of the Cornetto trilogy (and my dearly beloved Black Books) back in those days. 

**Retreat Through The Wet Wastelands (1973)**

Was in the mood for some horndoggery and picked this on a whim and I gotta say it was a good choice. It's very stylish and Takeo Chii is endlessly watchable in his bad cop role. Then again, there are no good cops in this movie. It's all about the police corruption in that small town and it's glorious. Though for some reason it felt like the film really wanted me to be empathetic to the bad cop who escapes from an insane asylum and has memory issues, but that's kinda hard to do with him being just as much a rapist as all the others




2. **Xeno** - A girl and her alien monster. Doesn’t exactly break new ground in the genre but is still an engaging watch, especially considering how low budget it is. The alien isn’t a cure pet. It’s a violent horror movie monster but isn’t inherently evil. I wish it leaned into the darkness. The dialogue is often atrocious. I still liked it overall.

**War Machine** - American military recruitment ad masquerading as a movie but it’s a lot of fun. Alan Ritchson carries the movie though I wish the other characters were fleshed out. You don’t need deep backstory. Just the right details could’ve made them memorable. Consider something like Predator. That we’re not asked to sympathise with the mental anguish of invading soldiers made this recruitment ad much more palatable to a non American viewer like me.

**Sambhavam** - This movie is to Caddo Lake what Caddo Lake is to Dark. A group of police officers in the Kerala-Tamil Nadu border enter a forbidden forest where time spirals in on itself. I wish it had better pacing and focused on an emotional core like Caddo Lake. It’s not a terrible movie but it’s very dry and mechanical. The characterisation is too thin.
3. ​**Pillion** (2025) \*\*\*\* A sub/dom romance told from the sub's point of view that keeps Skarsgard's dom character at arm's length and shows that devotion and obedience is not equal to love. While I appreciated exploring this unconventional relationship, I would have liked to have learned even more about the subculture and the other characters in it, and more about the barbershop quartet.

**Jane Austen's Period Drama** (2025) \*\*\* An amusing short that takes the period literally, but it's basically a *Key & Peele* level extended comedy sketch with a budget. 

**Hundreds of Beavers** (2022) \*\*\*\*\* My favorite comedy of the decade. 

**Avenue 5** Season 1 (2020) \*\*\* Armando Iannucci series about an interplanetary space cruise led by Hugh Laurie that dives right into the middle of things with little setup or explanation and takes a while for the mostly forced cringe humor to really click. I wasn't overly-enamoured with it, but they seem to have spent a fortune on sets and extras. It looks amazing for a British TV series.

**MacGruber** (2021) \*\*\* I never saw the movie (or any of the *Saturday Night Live* stuff where this apparently started) but the series was highly rated so I gave it a shot. It's an amusing action movie spoof with an overemphasis on crude jokes and not enough wit to carry it off (the basic difference between British and American humor). Will Forte is good, Laurence Fishburne isn't funny, and Kristen Wiig makes it all bearable. 

**Last Man Standing** (1996) \*\*\* Apparently if you soak your film stock in testosterone, it turns everything brown, and this is the brownest movie ever made. Walter Hill's macho remake of Kurosawa's Yojimbo is set in a Texas border town during prohibition. There's a lot of name dropping so it's hard to follow who is who and whom they keep referring to, but it boils down to Bruce Willis playing both sides of two rival gangs. It helps if you already know *Yojimbo*, which also takes a bit of figuring out. The overly dusty western town and Hill's typical truck commercial photography makes it western noir fantasy world that's almost a Lynchian dream. It's the kind of dad movie you stumble on at 2 a.m. when you can't sleep and in that context it's perfect. I guess you could say that about most Walter Hill films. 

**Little House: Look Back to Yesterday** (1983) \*\* Poor Albert! This post-series TV movie has Albert Ingalls dying from a blood disease just as he's about to enter college. The heavy subject doesn't leave a lot of room for humor, which is mostly handled by a corny school kid crush. The wholesomeness is palpable. I didn't realize how involved Michael Landon was in all of this. He wrote, produced, and directed multiple episodes of *Little House, Bonanza,* and *Highway to Heaven*. The guy was amazing.

**Terror in a Texas Town** (1958) \*\*\*\* This is a B-western with a tiny budget that's directed like a TV show, but none of that matters if you have a great script by a blacklisted Dalton Trumbo. It's an angry tale of tyranny and oppression by a writer who was experiencing it, and the storytelling is sharp if a little histrionic. Sterling Hayden is great as a Swedish immigrant with a bad accent, but the story spends a lot of time with its villain, terrifically played by Ned Young (who wrote *The Defiant Ones* and *Inherit the Wind*). 

**Tit for Tat** (1936) \*\*\*\* One of the better Laurel and Hardy shorts, where the comedic duo opens an electrical supply store and gets into a tussle with a neighboring business. Co-starring Mae Busch, who looks exactly like Teri Garr. Funny stuff!

**The Three Must-Get-Theres** (1922) \*\*\*\* ​My first Max Linder comedy is an *Airplane*\-styled spoof of *The Three Musketeers* (after the Douglas Fairbanks version from the year before) with a lot of great gags packed into a brief hour. I'll be watching more of his films. 
4. **Death Becomes Her (1992, Robert Zemeckis)**

I loved this one! It was fun to see Meryl Streep and Goldie Hawn together, the comedic timing was perfect. Also interesting to see Bruce Willis in a different type of role, he was great at playing a dorky surgeon. Just a fun watch overall. Reminded me of The Substance. **7.5/10**

**Picnic at Hanging Rock (1975, Peter Weir)**

Beautiful and enchanting film. The costumes, the score, the atmosphere, the characters… it really draws you in. Almost mesmerizing. Glad that I got to see a special showing of this in 4k. **8/10**
5. **Giant (1955)**

Absolutely wonderful epic drama. The first half or so is akin to a dramatic western, and the second is more of a family drama a la Magnificent Ambersons.

Elizabeth Taylor chews the scenery in her fantastic classic style, Rock Hudson as the slightly more nuanced lead we resent. But James Dean overshadows them both with the mumbly-Joe performance he got from the Strasberg method. Every character is sympathetically flawed, which lets us root for them to become better people rather than for their success.

The film is visually gorgeous, the composition of the interior shots even moreso than the sweaty, arid exterior cinematography. The sets are amazing, beautiful and very “lived in”.

The fading edits are used to transition to and from shots that last a couple seconds, which feels sorta lazy. The editing *within* any given scene is used brilliantly, especially with the reaction shots of stern-faced Hudson, but also the decisions of when *not* to cut and let us sit and watch to drink in the moment a bit longer.

**Train Dreams (2025)**

Train Dreams felt like reliving someone’s memories rather than being told a conventionally engaging story, which was cool. It’s not wholly satisfying, but that really fits the themes.

A couple bad CG moments took me out of it, but nothing I couldn’t shrug off; the effects for the trees more than made up for that.

The Kubrickian zoomouts were nice at first, then when I saw how the filmmakers were using those to define the visual theme, I really started to appreciate them. The lighting was amazing and the shutter angle/speed made it feel surreal, which was impressive since it usually makes the image feel more gritty and grounded.


6. Dersu Uzala (1975)

Later Kurosawa. I thought it was wonderful yet it was flickering the whole time apparently due to a bad transfer. 

However, there is a beautifully framed shot at dusk with Captain Arsenev and Dersu. We see the sun and moon together against a dark blue sky signifying the impending night. The Russian captain and his tripod stand on the left with the rising moon. Dersu and the setting sun on stand on the right. We see a beginning and an end both in man's world and in nature's side by side. It is a magnificently poignant shot that symbolically serves as the visual heart of the entire film. The timeless movements of a celestial dance mimicked below by mankind; incomprehensibly humbled. It sits high among the most stunning frames I've ever seen.

Near the end of the film, there's another carefully crafted mise-en-scène that captures the brutal march of modernization. Dersu has come to live in town with the captain and his family. He sits dejected, crushed by an imposed domesticity with his eyes fixed on the fireplace as the son happily plays piano, the captain reads the newspaper, and the lady of the house quietly knits. The family sits in a blissful state of calm while the Goldi hunter dreams of being back in the wild. The juxtaposition of imagery presented in this scene is devastating. Not a word spoken, yet all is clear. It's shots like these that fully realize the potential of film as a medium for visual storytelling. Kurosawa sure did know a thing or two.
7. **Platoon (1986, Oliver Stone) — re-watch** — In hindsight, I’m not really sure why I thought trying to watch *Platoon* for the third time was a good idea. Or, well, watching most of it for the third time, since, this time, I turned it off about 2/3rds of the way through. In any event, my opinion didn’t change much from the first two times I saw it; I still think it’s a good movie, but not a great one, and certainly not Best-Picture-worthy. Most of the movie’s most compelling parts relate to the two Sergeants, Elias and Barnes, and their conflicting philosophies; I think the movie would have been improved a lot if they had been the central focus throughout, instead of being treated as side characters to the much blander and less interesting Chris Taylor. Charlie Sheen being a much worse actor than either Dafoe or Berenger also doesn’t help. **7/10**

**Glory (1989, Edward Zwick)** — Speaking of movies not helped by poor casting choices, what the fuck is Matthew Broderick trying to do in this movie? The rest of the movie is fine, I guess — it’s a pretty typical, basic award-bait message movie, not really anything exceptional, but nothing that stands out as particularly inept, either — but Broderick is awful. Every time he attempts to show emotion, it just looks like he’s forgotten his line and is wondering why nobody’s reminded him yet. I guess I should give the movie some credit for at least having good intentions, but even that is undermined by its bizarre attempt at both-sidesing slavery, with a scene of a black former-slave recruit being slapped and berated by his (also black) commanding officer for being too prejudiced to be grateful for the sacrifices that the white Union soldiers are making for him. Even if the movie’s overall message is still obviously against slavery, it’s hard to imagine a Hollywood production treating a victim of an atrocity so condescendingly if the perpetrators had not been white Americans. **5/10**

**Transformers: Rise of the Beasts (2023, Steven Caple Jr.)** — Close to being done with the *Transformers* movies. *Rise of the Beasts* is probably the most forgettable and least noteworthy movie in the *Transformers* series, which also makes it one of the better ones. For once, the plot is reasonably coherent, the filmmaking is more-or-less competent, and the human protagonists, while still not particularly interesting characters, are at least not especially actively irritating. If it were the start of the series, I’d probably give it a middling 5 or 6 rating, but, being as it is the seventh movie, I do feel the need to dock some points for still basically being nothing but retreads of beats from earlier movies, even if slightly more competent in execution this time. Do not recommend. **4/10**

**Best movie of the week:** *Platoon*
8. [**WEEK # 270:**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)

*“Work is freedom”.*

**À NOUS LA LIBERTÉ** (1931) is my third classic film by René Clair (After 'I married a witch' and 'Entr’acte'). This is a satirical comedy about two escaped prisoners, one of which becomes fabulously rich as an owner of a gramophone factory.

It offered a sharp anti-capitalist critique of industrialization, assembly-line labor and conformity. The factory floor and forced prison work mirror each other, as both are inhuman mechanisms used to enslave the poor. It's also a partial musical (?!)

This film is noted because Chaplin's 'Modern Times' plagiarized it to a huge degree. Chaplin claimed that he never saw this one, and Carne was flattered that he might have been an inspiration for that film. But there were too many similarities between the two to ignore, especially in the visual descriptions of the conveyor belt scenes, the radical class struggles, and the anarchistic conclusion of leaving everything behind and walking into the open road.

I actually used to mistake René Clair for Marcel Carne, of 'Children of Paradise' fame. 💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes.

🍿

There's a buzz brewing at the moment because of a new MOMA presentation by Thierry Frémaux, a French film personality (and the director of Institut Lumière), [Lumière, Le Cinéma!](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_3BEsMtLhzU)

But this is actually his third collection of restored film clips which were made by Auguste and Louis Lumière. The inventors of the cinematograph, ushered in the birth of moving pictures during their first decade 1895-1905. **LUMIÈRE, THE ADVENTURE BEGINS** (2016) was the first part that Frémaux did (and the 2024 'Lumière! The Adventure Continues' was the sequel.)

It's a sober, straight-forward and delightful collection of 108 short films (In the beginning, they couldn't be longer than 50 seconds) out of the 1,500 films from the Lumière catalogue. Working with director and historian Bertrand Tavernier (and with a parting shot of Martin Scorsese!), he interprets each short clip, with compelling and informative passion. [**Must watch - 9/10.''](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EXXdq_RdBv0)

🍿

**2 BY CZECH DIRECTOR JAN HŘEBEJK:**

🍿 The period-piece comedy-drama **DIVIDED WE FALL** was nominated for Best Foreign Oscar in 2000. It tells of a childless Czech couple who hides a Jewish friend in their secret pantry, Anne Frank-style, during the Nazi occupation of World War 2.

It's a humane, somehow-nuanced depiction of non-heroes who fearfully fall into a bad situation where every line is gray, and no one is 100% good or evil. Spoiler alert: The wife ends up sleeping with the stowaway (for - reasons). [The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=NmhoECa04pc) 6/10.

Bonus for Bach's BWV 224 laid over the end titles.

🍿 *"Let me explain the difference between dumplings and Gnocchi".*

**COSY DENS ("PELÍŠKY")** (1999) is the highest-rated film on Letterboxd from Czechia, and a cultural mainstay universally beloved by many Czechs.

I can see why: It's pure nostalgia, a pleasant period piece comedy about a group of ordinary people during 1967, the last year of the 'Prague Spring'. A 16 yo son is infatuated with a beautiful teen, and all members of their middle class families are fantastically depicted. It's warm, funny, and a national classic. Apparently, if you're a 'real' Czech, you 'must' watch it on Christmas day every year.

🍿

*"Will you please take this injured look from your fat face!"*

**YOU WERE NEVER LOVELIER** (1942) was a second-tier musical with Fred Astaire and "Love Goddess" Rita Hayworth (one of only two films they made together). He is one of my favorite American actors of all time, and she was an extraordinarily ravishing woman. But this was a simple romantic comedy set up in the "exotic" location of Buenos Aires, Argentina, and their first dance together did not happen until the second part of the movie: There are only 3 dance numbers all together.

Items: Orchid-sending * father Adolphe Menjou * She with her monogrammed MA dress * and side-kick Xavier Cugat.

🍿

The new, unhinged time-loop fantasy **GOOD LUCK, HAVE FUN, DON’T DIE** is my first by Gore Verbinki (Not counting his [Budweiser Frogs](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SC5IqVOF19U) commercials!) Manic, full to the brim with dystopian creativity and unlimited mayhem. It stars Sam Rockwell as a 'man from the future' who must recruit the right number of night diners from Norm's in Los Angeles, to embark on a futile, lethal mission to stop an A.I. agent from destroying the planet.

It was described as "Terry Gilliam does The Terminator" (or maybe Black Mirror). And it offers some timely warnings and pseudo-critique about our tech-addiction, how desperately shitty life is today, and only getting worse. But at its core, it's a speculative science-fiction story, burdened with spectacular special effects, a Mid "Everything Everywhere All At Once" for a public suffering from an extremely short attention span.

[The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Nm4WbapDzDQ) 4/10.

🍿

*["I don't give a fuck about your war or your president!"](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/post/810017174797303809/snake-plissken)*

Because of this quote, I finally watched John Carpenter's brutal **ESCAPE FROM NEW YORK** (1981). Crime-ridden US had turned the whole island of Manhattan into a locked down high-security prison, POTUS Donald Pleasance was kidnapped when his airplane crashed inside, and badass Snake Plissken has 24 hours to save him, or a timed poison capsule injected into his veins will be activated and kill him. Excellent Carpenter 80s synth score. 7/10.

🍿

After seeing a cluster of talky dramas by Hong Sang-soo, I took a few years break, because, in all honesty, his films ended up feeling redundant.

**BY THE STREAM** (2024) is my 12th pleasant Korean film by him, and again, it's a quiet, improvisational chamber play, with a simple atmospheric story, hesitant acting and players, eating and drinking in small restaurants [This time they enjoy [Tteokbokki](https://duckduckgo.com/?t=ffab&q=tteokbokki&atb=v397-1&ia=images&iax=images)], but no earth-shuttering revelations. The final sentence uttered *“There’s nothing to see…”* actually sums up pretty well the whole mood.

Pretty art teacher Kim MIn-mee invites her renowned ex-actor uncle to write a skit at her university, after a scandal with the previous director. The stakes are minor, but so are the interactions. 💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes.

🍿

**2 MORE WITH CATHERINE DENEUVE:**

🍿 *"We'll be very happy. And we'll always be in love."*

because of [this opening scene](https://www.artofthetitle.com/title/the-umbrellas-of-cherbourg/), I took another frequent re-watch of one of my all-time favorites, Demy's **THE UMBRELLAS OF CHERBOURG** (1964) ♻️. The prettiest 19 yo woman in my world [[She was gorgeous even as a child](https://old.reddit.com/r/classicfilms/comments/1rk8vwr/child_actress_catherine_deneuve_1952/)] in a tragic romance, with Michel Legrand score doing all the heavy lifting.

Observations: Nobody bothered to lock their bicycles in 1957 * It rained a lot over there by the coast * The Roland Cassard character is actually a continuation of a role the same actor played in Demy's 'Lola' (!) * She left her daughter in the car with the window rolled down in the freezing snow * Most notably is the blatant arranged marriage agreement set up by Geneviève's mother, determined to get her daughter a 'good husband' while still young. The reason that the mother used is because she's "not exceptionally beautiful"... *Hahahaha..*




Mandatory clips: [The trailer](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=imCOi7pqKh0) and the [train station scene.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qfafEc2E8DU) **Always 10/10.**

🍿 **DON'T TOUCH THE WHITE WOMAN** is my 3rd slapstick Western by Italian provocateur Marco Ferreri. The set-up was promising, like 'Blazing Saddles' but in French: It's a fictionalized version of Custer's Last Stand, shot in 1974's Paris, on the background of the real-life demolition of Les Halles Market. A black comedy, satirical and cynical critique of colonialism, mixing politics and absurdist sensibilities, it starred Marcello Mastroianni and then-wife Catherine Deneuve, as well as the rest of the guys from 'Le Grande Bouffe'. But it was impossible to sit through. ⬇️ Could Not Finish it ⬇️.

🍿

**FROM MEIR, TO MEIR** (2021) is an endearing personal Upper-Egyptian journey. The filmmaker traveled 400 kilometers south of Cairo to the village (now a small town), where her family came from, and to which they still feel a strong bond, even many decades after moving away. She documents the hardships and simple customs of the poor people who still live there. Like elsewhere around the world, there's no "future" in the province, and practically everybody just wants to leave and go someplace else. (Also, suicides there are very prevalent!). Life is dusty in Meir and highly relatable. I loved it. 7/10. *[Female Director]*

A very similar story: **WHEN IT'S GOOD, IT'S GOOD** (2023), a 10-min documentary about another tiny dot on the map. Denver City is a small boom-and-bust oil town in West Texas. Its population is 4470, and it has 1 grocery store, 5 gas stations, 1 school, 8 restaurants - and 12 churches. The filmmaker comes back to her hometown, and interviews some of the people there. It's a must watch. **8/10.** *[Female Director]*

## 4. Movies with Subway /Train /Metro Scenes

**Score:** 3
**Body:** Hey everyone! I’m looking for movies or TV shows recommendations that feature a lot of scenes in subways, metros, or trains. Anything where the underground transit system or trains are used frequently or play a noticeable role in the story or atmosphere. It can be action, romance, thriller, drama, or anything else, as long as there are plenty of subway/train moments.
Older or newer productions are all welcome.
Thanks in advance for any suggestions

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The daddy of them all is probably [The Taking Of Pelham One Two Three](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Taking_of_Pelham_One_Two_Three_(1974_film)) which was filmed with the co-operation of the NYC transit authority, with extensive location shooting on trains, in tunnels, and control centre infrastructure of the NYC subway. The story is about a train hijacking and hostage situation. 

Of note also is [The Warriors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Warriors_(film)) which makes use of the NYC subway and elevated rail lines as key parts of the story, following a group of gang members marked for death by their enemies and hunted by police. The film was also shot on location across NYC.

May I also recommend [Style Wars](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Style_Wars), a documentary about graffiti painters in NYC made during the heyday of hip hop culture. A large part of the graffiti culture involved breaking into railyards to paint the trains, and then tracking and photographing the trains before the TA had time to clean off the paint. You also see the frustrated transit cops trying to prevent this from happening.

Enjoy 
2. [**Metropia**](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4wz7jV4peDw) (2009) by Swedish-Egyptian director Tarik Saleh is one of the most innovative and unusual film I’ve never heard of. A complex dystopic story which uses an uncanny hybrid of photoshoped photography and animation. An oppressive story about a paranoiac Moby-lookalike man who hears voices in his head and stumbles upon a European conspiracy related to a massive Metro train network. But it’s so grey & so strange! Highly recommended.
3. Two of my favourite movies that, coincidentally, also employ the subway theme very successfully:

Subway (1985, an early Luc Besson film which is set almost entirely in the Parisian metro, with Christopher Lambert and Isabelle Adjani in the main roles) and Kontroll (2003, Hungarian, directed by Nimrod Antal).
4. Jacques Rivette's *Secret Defense* turns train travel (both Metro and long-haul) into a key focus of its detective procedural. 

*News From Home* has a lot of scenes in the NYC metro. But not utilized in a traditional narrative film style. 

Anthony Asquith's 1928 silent thriller *Underground* takes the London Underground as one of its central locations. 

Michael Mann's *Collateral* has one of the more iconic metro scenes (extra funny because it's the LA Metro, which is so underutilized).
5. I have been working my way through all available 19th century "films" and have I got 600-800 short films for you!

Aside from that, The Darjeeling Limited and 2022's Bullet Train easily come to mind. 

1976's Silver Streak doesn't get talked about enough when it comes to Gene Wilder comedies and takes place on the LA to Chicago train.

The Cowboy Bebop movie, Knocking on Heaven's Door, has a big train fight scene that leads to a derailment. 

And in a complete change of vibe, Shoah talks pretty extensively about trains and includes a "phantom ride" on one of them.

## 5. Brothers (2009) fails to do its tragic premise justice

**Score:** 2
**Body:** Was thinking about the movie Brothers the other day, the one with Tobey Maguire. And while it wasn’t that good for a few reasons, I was always fascinated by the premise. And I was thinking about how the film and script let down what is a really interesting Greek tragedy level premise. 

The story is essentially a Greek tragedy about a man who made the wrong choice. Hes a soldier captured in the desert, he’s given a choice, to kill his friend so he can return home to his family, or die himself. He chooses to kill and therefore gets to return home

Only when he does get home he finds that the world had already moved on from him, and everyone would be better off if he had died out there. His wife had emotionally moved on and his brother has stepped up to the role he left empty. The world has poetically restructured around his absence and his return is unwelcome, and made even worse by the moral betrayal he committed, in the name of seeing the wife who no longer loves him and a daughter who now hates him. 

That’s a dark concept, so the film does the Hollywood thing of giving it an optimistic ending where he is forgiven. This is fine, but it would’ve been a lot stronger if the film was an out and out tragedy, in my opinion. It feels pedestrian and like it didn’t understand the darkness of its own premise, instead just devolving into a pretty standard drama. 

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think this is misreading the movie. It's called Brothers for a reason, both brothers have important character arcs. The younger brother has always been seen as an irresponsible loser, but when he's needed he steps up and assumes responsibility. In a kind of mirror development the older brother has always been seen as the grown up, self-reliant one, but when he has to deal with a trauma so severe, that it's tearing him apart, he has to realise, that he can't make it without help from his family and community. So it's not really a tragedy it's a story about the potential for growth you have, if you're allowed to grow.
  - You’re talking about the film as it is. I’m talking about what I think it should’ve been. That’s different. Im saying that I find the moral dilemma far more interesting than the brother drama.
    - The movie was never a tragedy. In a tragedy, the fatal flaws of the characters inevitably dooms them from the outset, this is not the case in this movie at all. The older brother would have had a wonderful life with is loving wife, if he wasn't captured in the desert and forced to kill his friend. 
2. The Scandinavian film is superior.

There are a lot more shadings to the world and its characters that make you feel you are witnessing the lived experiences of flesh-and-blood people.

Sheridan's film is too polished, too Hollywood and I don't think any of the actors are at their best (and Maguire is actively awful).

I don't see an issue with the ending (which is close to the original film).

I just don't believe it's a tragedy or intended to be.

The soldier doesn't possess a tragic flaw that is intrinsic to his character.

He is a man undergoing trauma and I don't think a tragic ending would have supported that story.


3. Watch the Danish original! It’s really good and it’s got the perpetually underrated Ulrich Thomsen in it. It doesn’t end the way you’re saying you’d prefer, but it doesn’t really feel like it’s got a standard Hollywood kinda tone either
